[2018-02-22 22:52] crash_overlord: Also prismata kinda guides you into the openings

[2018-02-22 22:52] crash_overlord: all the "natural" lines are... well, natural
[2018-02-22 22:53] mqp: i don't think there is much of a difference, and that's after spending tens of thousands of hours on chess and go -- those games guide you into the openings as well via principles like local shape and harmonious piece development
[2018-02-22 22:54] crash_overlord: But those things are much harder to grasp then "Have a 0 at the bottom"
[2018-02-22 22:54] mqp: i agree that in many prismata sets the best openings are sort of trivial to discover, but i do think there is a good 25% of sets or so where the best opening is not obvious but could well be known by experience (and it matters if you do it)

[2018-02-22 22:57] crash_overlord: and that play/experience will develop into skill naturally
[2018-02-22 22:57] crash_overlord: While yes the same is true for chess and go, theres a very real perception that that is not the case.

[2018-02-22 22:58] crash_overlord: And we don't care about the technical truth that the 0.0001% of people that are really good at chess and go and prismata will understand, but what it looks like and sounds like to the general demographic

--- Thread (1 messages, 2018-02-25) ---
[2018-02-25 13:40] steel0229e (Steel): @Juuzou  the only way to improve at this game is to play. Guides are only helpful once you have a few hundred games under your belt and you start to develop a basic game sense. Most of the concepts are so advanced that you would not benefit from reading them. The Standard Style is the best noob guide out there on Prismata Library. Other than that, try to beat master bot and go through the combat training missions (skip the harder ones you can't deal with yet). I don't recommend trying to mimic top players because their strategy requires perfect micro decisions and execution and the builds are usually very tight and reactive to the opponent. Instead just try to improve in small steps, like getting familiar with all the units, learning how to use the absorb and granularity exploit mechanics etc. Trying to win in PvP is a futile attempt until next month where there will be more beginners. This game is super hardcore.

--- Thread (50 messages, 2018-02-28) ---
[2018-02-28 02:03] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): (continued from other channel discussion)
prismata is most accurately a board game played on the computer themed after RTS games, having the multiple resource types, economy, and military unit mix-and-match strategy focus.
[2018-02-28 02:05] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): appearance-wise, it has many similarities with computer-based card games, but without having most of the mechanics involved in a card game, i'd not really call it a card game in any way, gameplay-wise
[2018-02-28 02:05] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): there's no luck of the draw, no deckbuilding, no random effects, very little 1v1 card vs card interaction, and so on
[2018-02-28 02:06] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): it's the perfect game for a macro-type RTS player, too
[2018-02-28 02:06] randomflyingtaco (RiFT (#RIPBrowserers)): appearance-wise?  I'm not sure I follow
[2018-02-28 02:06] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): if you don't have the unit training tab visible, it has a board with card-shaped things on it
[2018-02-28 02:06] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): and an end turn button
[2018-02-28 02:06] jamesa7171: if you asked someone who wasn't familiar with Prismata to guess what it was based on a picture, I feel like 99% of people would say "card game"
[2018-02-28 02:07] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): the unit training tab might be your hand, dunno
[2018-02-28 02:07] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): to some uninitiated person
[2018-02-28 02:07] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): it reminds me most of old C&C though
[2018-02-28 02:07] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): and old C&C was the best.
[2018-02-28 02:08] jamesa7171: but anyway @mqp to continue the discussion from the other chat, claiming that prismata _is_ a card game would certainly be terrible for various reasons, but pointing out that the gameplay experience is similar to what you would experience if you were playing a card game is both accurate and helpful from a sales standpoint (though I feel the original point is getting lost in this discussion)
[2018-02-28 02:09] mqp: this is totally unrelated but it's clear that everyone else in the universe has a different definition of "card game" than me
[2018-02-28 02:09] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): but i think while mentioning it might appeal to CCG players is a good idea, since that appears to be true, i'd distance it from them in the description, since i feel it lacks pretty much everything that separates card games from non-card-games in mechanics
[2018-02-28 02:09] wilson0862 (Wilson): So what's a card game mq.
[2018-02-28 02:10] wilson0862 (Wilson): I imagine you are explaining so my asking was unnecessary.
[2018-02-28 02:10] jamesa7171: yeah i agree with that @Kynareth No Baka, the comparison has to be done delicately
[2018-02-28 02:10] mqp: but like, any random game played with cards? they are all different? calling something a "video game" or "board game" doesn't have any very specific connotations to me
[2018-02-28 02:10] mqp: "card game" is the same
[2018-02-28 02:11] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): because it will DEFINITELY appeal to non-CCG players - even people who _loathe_ CCGs
[2018-02-28 02:11] mqp: netrunner, MTG, dominion, poker, bridge don't have a lot of things in common
[2018-02-28 02:11] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): do they all have luck in them?
[2018-02-28 02:12] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): there's no luck in prismata
[2018-02-28 02:13] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): the reason i pointed out luck immediately is that the person who i tried to get to play prismata who was turned away by the CCG association mentioned hatred of luck based games, on account of them allowing someone to win without skill.
[2018-02-28 02:13] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): and that's a key difference in prismata. you won't win by being lucky
[2018-02-28 02:13] randomflyingtaco (RiFT (#RIPBrowserers)): the closest it comes to card games is that the board state looks similar to mtg
[2018-02-28 02:14] mqp: that's fair and for all i know people associate card games with luck
[2018-02-28 02:14] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): i know that skill plays a huge role too
[2018-02-28 02:15] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): but luck still exists - if you don't draw a key card, you'll lose, no matter how well thought out your prior plays and deckbuilding was
[2018-02-28 02:16] kynarethnobaka (Kynareth No Baka): in prismata, how well you thought things out and how well you react to your opponent is _all that matters_ - it rewards skill with wins in a practically 1:1 ratio
[2018-02-28 02:16] mqp: incidentally, i'm surprised if a lot of people really choose games based on the quantity of luck involved, i feel like i don't notice the kinds of games i and my friends like being clumped on the luck/no-luck axis
[2018-02-28 02:16] mqp: seems like a lot of other things are more salient

messages.author: kynarethnobaka
messages.content:
(continued from other channel discussion)
prismata is most accurately a board game played on the computer themed after RTS games, having the multiple resource types, economy, and military unit mix-and-match strategy focus.

messages.author: kynarethnobaka
messages.content: appearance-wise, it has many similarities with computer-based card games, but without having most of the mechanics involved in a card game, i'd not really call it a card game in any way, gameplay-wise

messages.author: kynarethnobaka
messages.content: there's no luck of the draw, no deckbuilding, no random effects, very little 1v1 card vs card interaction, and so on

messages.author: kynarethnobaka
messages.content: it's the perfect game for a macro-type RTS player, too
